alt: The mathematical formula for the softmax function, showing e to the power of x_j divided by the sum of e to the power of x_i for all i.
description: Equation defining softmax for a vector x: softmax(x)_j = exp(x_j) / sum_{i=1}^n exp(x_i).
teaching_value: Gives the exact baseline definition needed to reason about normalization dependence on all entries and why naive computation is not streamable.
when_to_show: Show when introducing softmax or before discussing numerical stability and streaming/online reformulations.

alt: The mathematical formula showing that subtracting a constant 'c' from each element of the input vector to softmax does not change the result.
description: Derivation showing softmax invariance to shifting inputs by a constant c: softmax(x)_j = exp(x_j-c) / sum_i exp(x_i-c), so subtracting c cancels.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the key numerical-stability trick (subtract max) that prevents overflow/underflow and is later reused in streaming softmax updates.
when_to_show: Show right before implementing stable softmax or explaining why subtracting max(x) is valid and necessary.
suggested_caption: Softmax is unchanged by subtracting a constant (Seth Weidman).

alt: The attention formula with a red box highlighting the softmax of the scaled dot-product of Q and K-transpose, which is then multiplied by V.
description: Scaled dot-product attention equation with a highlighted softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d)) term, followed by multiplication by V.
teaching_value: Connects the abstract 'softmax-dot' operation to the concrete attention pipeline, motivating why streaming softmax-dot matters for FlashAttention-style tiling.
when_to_show: Show when motivating streaming softmax-dot from attention, or when mapping math symbols (Q,K,V) to the softmax and weighted-sum steps.
suggested_caption: Softmax in attention: weights from QKᵀ then applied to V (Seth Weidman).

description: A line chart comparing inference latency on OpenLLaMA 1.4B across methods, showing that StableMask adapted for inference (SM-I) runs faster (lower latency) than baselines, consistent with window-based KV caching without preserving initial tokens.
teaching_value: Helps students connect an algorithmic masking change (fixing DA tokens/attention sink) to a measurable systems outcome: reduced decoding latency during long-context generation with sliding-window attention and KV cache truncation.
when_to_show: Show after introducing attention sinks and why StableMask enables dropping early tokens; use it when discussing practical deployment tradeoffs (speed vs. long-context stability) and motivating SM-I for fast decoding.
suggested_caption: SM-I reduces OpenLLaMA-1.4B inference latency (StableMask, arXiv 2402.04779).

description: A schematic illustrating the DA (disproportionate attention) issue under causal masking and the proposed fix: adding pseudo-attention scores to positions that are forced to zero by the causal mask, so the model learns not to over-focus on low-information regions where the conditional entropy H(X_{n+1}|Ω^{(l)}_{≤i}=ω^{(l)}_{≤i}) is high.
teaching_value: Makes the proof intuition concrete: causal masking creates systematic zeroed entries that can distort softmax attention; pseudo-scores stabilize the distribution and discourage attention to uninformative contexts, clarifying why StableMask improves extrapolation and avoids DA tokens.
when_to_show: Show while walking through the proof/definition of disproportionality of attention; pause to ask learners to identify which entries are zeroed by the causal mask and how adding pseudo-scores changes the softmax denominator.
suggested_caption: DA tokens and pseudo-score fix under causal masking (StableMask, arXiv 2402.04779).

description: Schematic of attention over a word sequence: a query vector is compared (dot products) to each word’s key to produce weights, which are then used to compute a weighted average of the corresponding value vectors, yielding an output representation.
teaching_value: Makes the abstract Q/K/V idea concrete by showing how similarity scores become weights and how those weights mix information across tokens to form a context-aware representation.
when_to_show: Show right after introducing queries, keys, and values, before presenting the matrix-form scaled dot-product attention equation.
suggested_caption: Attention: query scores keys to weight values (UvA Deep Learning Course).

description: Block diagram of scaled dot-product attention: matrices Q and K are multiplied to form a score matrix, scaled by 1/sqrt(d_k), passed through softmax to obtain attention probabilities, then multiplied by V to produce the attended outputs.
teaching_value: Connects the formula to computation steps, clarifying tensor shapes and why scaling stabilizes softmax when d_k is large.
when_to_show: Use when transitioning from intuitive attention to the efficient batched/matrix implementation and discussing numerical stability.
suggested_caption: Scaled dot-product attention computation pipeline (UvA Deep Learning Course).

description: Multi-head attention diagram: input Q/K/V are linearly projected into multiple heads, each head performs scaled dot-product attention independently, head outputs are concatenated, then projected with a final linear layer.
teaching_value: Shows how multiple attention subspaces let the model attend to different relationships simultaneously and how concatenation/projection recombines them.
when_to_show: Show after students understand single-head attention, when motivating why one attention distribution can be insufficient.
suggested_caption: Multi-head attention: parallel heads then concat + projection (UvA Deep Learning Course).

description: Comparison graphic contrasting convolutional networks, recurrent networks, and self-attention in terms of how information flows across positions (e.g., path length/dependency distance and parallelism), highlighting self-attention’s permutation-equivariant set-like behavior without positional encoding.
teaching_value: Helps students reason about inductive biases and computational tradeoffs: locality in CNNs, sequential dependence in RNNs, and global interactions/parallelism in attention—plus why positional information must be injected.
concepts: inductive bias (locality vs recurrence vs global attention)
when_to_show: Use when discussing why Transformers differ from CNNs/RNNs and to motivate positional encodings for order-sensitive tasks.
suggested_caption: CNN vs RNN vs self-attention information flow (UvA Deep Learning Course).

description: Transformer encoder architecture diagram: token embeddings are combined with positional encodings, passed through stacked encoder blocks containing multi-head self-attention and a position-wise feed-forward network, with residual connections and layer normalization around sublayers.
teaching_value: Provides the big-picture map of where attention sits in the model and how residuals/normalization/FFN compose into a repeatable encoder layer.
when_to_show: Show after explaining multi-head attention, when assembling components into a full encoder layer and discussing stacking depth.
suggested_caption: Transformer encoder block with self-attention and FFN (UvA Deep Learning Course).

description: Plot/visualization of sinusoidal positional encoding values across sequence positions for selected hidden dimensions, showing sine for even indices and cosine for odd indices with varying wavelengths.
teaching_value: Makes the sinusoidal design intuitive by revealing phase shifts between paired dimensions and how wavelengths grow across dimensions to encode both short- and long-range position patterns.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the positional encoding formula to connect symbols (pos, i) to actual numeric patterns.
suggested_caption: Sinusoidal positional encodings across positions and dimensions (UvA Deep Learning Course).

description: Heatmap image of positional encoding matrix with position on one axis and hidden dimension on the other; color indicates the added feature value, revealing repeating sinusoidal bands with increasing periods for higher dimensions.
teaching_value: Lets students visually compare frequencies across dimensions and see how the encoding provides unique position signatures while remaining smooth and structured.
when_to_show: Use after implementing positional encoding to validate understanding and to discuss how different dimensions capture different scales.
suggested_caption: Heatmap of sinusoidal positional encoding matrix (UvA Deep Learning Course).

description: Line plot comparing training behavior with and without learning-rate warm-up (e.g., loss vs iterations/steps), illustrating that warm-up stabilizes early training while no warm-up can diverge or converge worse.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the practical optimization impact of warm-up, motivating it as a necessary training trick for deep Transformers rather than a cosmetic schedule choice.
when_to_show: Show when students ask why warm-up is used or when presenting recommended Transformer training recipes and failure modes.
suggested_caption: Warm-up improves early Transformer training stability (after Liu et al., 2019).

description: Grid of attention heatmaps arranged by transformer layer (rows) and attention head (columns) for a sequence-to-sequence toy task, showing strong weights near the anti-diagonal corresponding to attending to the flipped-index token.
teaching_value: Helps students interpret multi-head self-attention by linking heatmap structure (anti-diagonal) to the learned algorithm (sequence reversal) and reinforcing that softmax is row-wise (each query distributes probability over keys).
when_to_show: After introducing attention matrices and head/layer dimensions; use it to ask learners to identify what pattern indicates the model learned reversal and why nearby tokens also receive probability mass.
suggested_caption: Attention maps by layer/head reveal learned sequence reversal (UvA DLC).

description: Example input set for set anomaly detection: a collection of CIFAR-100 images where one image differs in class from the others (the anomaly).
teaching_value: Makes the set anomaly task concrete by showing the unordered input and the single out-of-distribution element the model must identify, motivating permutation-equivariant architectures over RNNs.
when_to_show: Before discussing why transformers suit sets; use it to prompt students to define the learning objective (pick the odd element) and why order should not matter.
suggested_caption: Set anomaly example: one image differs from the rest (UvA DLC).

description: Multi-layer, multi-head attention heatmaps for a set of 10 images in the anomaly task, showing several heads concentrating attention on the anomalous (last) image while others distribute attention across the set.
teaching_value: Shows how different heads specialize: some act as anomaly detectors by focusing on the outlier, while others aggregate global context, helping students connect attention patterns to model behavior and interpretability.
when_to_show: After presenting model predictions; use it to compare heads that lock onto the anomaly versus heads that average across elements, and to discuss how layers change communication patterns.
suggested_caption: Attention heads highlight the anomalous set element (UvA DLC).

description: Another panel of attention heatmaps across layers/heads for the same set anomaly example, emphasizing that later-layer heads can assign very low attention to the anomaly while earlier heads may focus on it.
teaching_value: Helps students reason about layer-wise processing: early layers may detect the odd element, while later layers may down-weight it to form a consistent representation of the normal subset, illustrating hierarchical refinement in transformers.
when_to_show: When explaining why multiple layers are useful; ask learners to interpret why some heads ignore the anomaly and how that could support classification of the outlier index.
suggested_caption: Later layers may suppress attention to the anomaly (UvA DLC).
